name: Castillo de la Peña - circular trailrunning desde Martos
region_intro: Jogging around Martos offers diverse terrain within the province of Jaén, Andalusia. The city's elevated position provides natural inclines and declines, with routes often featuring panoramic views of the surrounding "sea of olives." Runners can explore extensive olive groves, historical sites, and trails leading towards the Sierra Jabalcuz mountain range. The region's landscape is characterized by prominent hills, agricultural land, and remnants of ancient railway lines repurposed for outdoor activities. Best jogging routes around Martos The most popular jogging route is Castillo de la Peña - circular trailrunning desde Martos, a difficult 3.5 miles (5.7 km) trail that takes 45 minutes to complete. This route ascends to the historic castle atop La Peña, offering commanding views of the area. Another top favourite among local runners is Old Martos Station loop from Martos, a moderate 4.6 miles (7.4 km) path. This trail follows relatively flat sections, likely incorporating parts of the Vía Verde del Aceite, through the olive groves. Local runners also love the Running loop from Antigua Estacion Tren de Martos, a 9.7 miles (15.5 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 1 hour 45 minutes. This longer route provides a challenging experience with significant elevation changes. Jogging around Martos is defined by hilly terrain, vast olive groves, and access to the Sierra Jabalcuz mountain range. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging trail running. The routes in Martos are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from 8 reviews. More than 790 runners have used komoot to explore Martos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Martos?
A: Martos offers a diverse selection of over a dozen running routes, catering to various fitness levels. You'll find a mix of moderate and difficult trails, with 11 routes rated as moderate and 3 as difficult, ensuring there's something for every runner.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when running in Martos?
A: Jogging in Martos provides a varied landscape. You can expect significant hilly terrain due to the city's elevated position, offering natural inclines and declines. Many routes traverse the expansive 'sea of olives' – endless olive groves – and some provide access to the Sierra Jabalcuz mountain range, ensuring a mix of agricultural and mountainous backdrops.

Q: Are there challenging trail running options in Martos?
A: Yes, Martos is an excellent location for challenging trail running. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989221301/castillo-de-la-pena-circular-trailrunning-desde-martos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de la Peña - circular trailrunning desde Martos&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 3.5-mile (5.7 km) route, ascend to the historic castle atop La Peña, providing significant elevation gain and commanding views. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21846411" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of the Peña of Martos loop from Martos&lt;/a&gt;, which features over 270 meters of elevation gain.

Q: Can I find routes that follow the Vía Verde del Aceite?
A: Absolutely! The Vía Verde del Aceite (Oil Greenway) is a prominent feature for runners in Martos. This repurposed old railway line offers relatively flat and accessible sections through the olive groves. You can find highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1813618" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beautiful section of the Vía Verde del Aceite&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4751216" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Start of the Vía Verde del Aceite&lt;/a&gt;, which are ideal for scenic jogs. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31406668" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Martos Station loop from Martos&lt;/a&gt; likely incorporates parts of this greenway.

Q: What historical sites or natural landmarks can I see while jogging in Martos?
A: Martos's routes are rich with historical and natural landmarks. You can jog towards the prominent La Peña, a large rocky mass with a ruined castle, offering panoramic views. The city itself has remnants of its old town walls and the Torre del Homenaje. The Vía Verde del Aceite also features historical railway infrastructure like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4955944" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viaduct of the Piedra del Águila Stream&lt;/a&gt;. Nearby, you might also encounter other castles such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5286708" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Santa Catalina&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there circular running routes in Martos?
A: Yes, several routes in Martos are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989221301/castillo-de-la-pena-circular-trailrunning-desde-martos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de la Peña - circular trailrunning desde Martos&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you up to the castle and back. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/27626142" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Martos&lt;/a&gt;, offering a moderate 7.7 km experience.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Martos?
A: The running routes in Martos are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 4.6 stars from 8 reviews. Runners often praise the varied terrain, the stunning panoramic views, and the unique experience of jogging through the extensive olive groves and past historical sites.

Q: Is parking available near the running trails in Martos?
A: While specific parking details for every trailhead are not always listed, Martos, as a city, generally offers parking options. For routes that start from the city center or near the old train station (Antigua Estacion Tren de Martos), you can typically find street parking or designated areas. For sections of the Vía Verde del Aceite, there are often parking spots near the old station buildings along the route.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in Martos?
A: The best time for jogging in Martos is generally during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these seasons, the weather is milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, sometimes wetter, conditions of winter. This allows for more comfortable and enjoyable runs amidst the olive groves and hilly landscapes.

Q: Are there any easy running options for beginners or casual joggers?
A: While Martos is known for its hilly terrain, there are moderate options suitable for beginners or casual joggers looking for a less strenuous experience. Routes that incorporate sections of the Vía Verde del Aceite, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31406668" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Martos Station loop from Martos&lt;/a&gt;, tend to be flatter and more accessible, winding through the scenic olive groves.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Martos?
A: Generally, many outdoor trails in Spain, especially those in natural areas like olive groves or greenways, are dog-friendly. However, it's always advisable to keep your dog on a leash, especially in agricultural areas or near historical sites, and to be mindful of local regulations regarding waste disposal. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and doesn't disturb wildlife or other trail users.

Q: Are there family-friendly jogging routes in Martos?
A: For families, routes that are relatively flat and well-maintained are ideal. Sections of the Vía Verde del Aceite are particularly suitable, as they are often paved or have a smooth gravel surface, making them safe for children and less challenging. These routes also offer interesting sights like old railway stations and viaducts, which can keep younger family members engaged.
tips: Ruins of an old castle built in the 14th century by the Order of Calatrava in the Spanish city of Martos, Jaén. It was built on the ruins of another ancient Arab fortress, which in turn had been built on the ruins of another primitive Ibero-Roman fortification.

waypoints.name: Castle of the Peña of Martos
